suggestions.name: FileContentReader
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on reading file contents and converting them to strings with different charset handling options. Extracting them into a FileContentReader class improves separation of concerns by isolating file reading logic from other file operations, while maintaining high cohesion around the single responsibility of file content extraction.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, focused responsibility: reading the complete contents of a file and converting it into a String representation. All three methods are overloaded variants that handle different charset specifications (default charset, Charset object, or charset name string) but accomplish the same core task of file-to-string conversion.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods represent the "file reading" domain concept, specifically focused on text file content extraction. They form a cohesive unit that abstracts the complexity of opening files, handling character encoding, ensuring proper resource cleanup, and converting binary file data into string format. This is a classic example of related functionality that operates on the same data type (File) to produce the same output type (String).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates file reading logic from other file operations (writing, copying, moving, etc.)
- Code Reusability: A dedicated FileReader class can be easily reused across different components that need to read text files
- Complexity Reduction: Removes file reading concerns from the large FileUtils class, making it more focused
- Testability: File reading logic can be independently tested and mocked more easily

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate file reading concerns from general file utilities. The extraction creates a specialized component with high cohesion (all methods read files to strings) and low coupling (minimal dependencies, only requires File and charset handling). This aligns with the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class dedicated solely to file content reading operations.

suggestions.name: ByteSizeFormatter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for converting byte counts into human-readable display strings with appropriate units. They share formatting logic, unit constants, and have a single clear responsibility of byte size presentation. Extracting them into a ByteSizeFormatter improves separation of concerns by isolating formatting logic from file operations.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: converting byte counts into human-readable display strings with appropriate units (QB, RB, YB, ZB, EB, PB, TB, GB, MB, KB, bytes). All three methods accomplish the same task but accept different input types (BigInteger, long, Number) and delegate to the core BigInteger implementation.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "byte size formatting" domain concept. They encapsulate the knowledge of byte unit hierarchies, conversion logic, and display formatting rules. The associated constants define the unit boundaries, and the methods implement the conversion algorithm with appropriate unit selection and formatting.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates byte size formatting logic from general file utilities
- Code Reusability: The formatter can be used by any class needing human-readable byte sizes, not just file operations
- Complexity Reduction: Removes formatting concerns from the FileUtils class, making it more focused on actual file operations
- Testability: Formatting logic can be tested independently with various edge cases and input types

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate formatting concerns from file manipulation logic. The extracted class represents a pure formatter with high cohesion (all methods format byte sizes) and low coupling (no dependencies on file system operations). This aligns with the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class dedicated solely to byte size display formatting.

suggestions.name: LineWriter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on writing collections as lines to files, with various parameter combinations for encoding, line endings, and append modes. Extracting them into a LineWriter class creates a focused, reusable component that encapsulates the specific responsibility of line-based file output operations.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: writing collections of data as lines to files. All eight methods are overloaded variants of `writeLines` that take a collection of objects, convert them to strings using `toString()`, and write them line-by-line to a file. They handle different combinations of parameters (charset, line endings, append mode) but accomplish the same core task.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "line-based file writing" domain. They represent a cohesive abstraction for converting collections to line-delimited text files. This is a specific subset of file I/O operations focused on structured text output where each collection element becomes a separate line in the output file.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Separates line-writing logic from general file utilities
- Enhances Reusability: The line writer can be used independently and potentially extended for other line-based formats
- Reduces Complexity: Removes 8 overloaded methods from the FileUtils god class, making it more focused
- Improves Testability: Line writing logic can be tested in isolation with specific test cases for different parameter combinations

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a cohesive set of related operations. The methods form a natural abstraction around "writing collections as lines to files" which is a common pattern in data export, logging, and report generation scenarios. The extraction creates high cohesion (all methods serve the same purpose) and maintains low coupling (minimal dependencies on the original class).

suggestions.name: FileLineReader
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused exclusively on reading file contents line by line into String collections. Extracting them into a FileLineReader class isolates this specific file reading responsibility from the broader FileUtils class, improving maintainability and allowing for independent reuse of line-reading functionality.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, focused responsibility: reading file contents line by line and returning them as a List of Strings. All three methods are overloaded variants that handle different charset specifications (default charset, Charset object, or charset name string) but accomplish the same core task of line-by-line file reading.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods represent the "line-based file reading" domain concept. They form a cohesive unit focused specifically on parsing files into line collections, as opposed to other file operations like writing, copying, or byte-level reading. This is a specialized subset of file I/O operations.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates line-reading logic from other file utilities
- Code reusability: LineReader can be used independently across different contexts
- Complexity reduction: Removes specialized reading logic from the general FileUtils class
- Testability: Line reading behavior can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns based on the Interface Segregation Principle. The extracted class will have high cohesion (all methods read lines from files) and low coupling (minimal dependencies beyond standard I/O classes).

suggestions.name: FileWriter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for writing various data types (strings, character sequences, byte arrays) to files with different encoding and append options. Extracting them into a FileWriter class creates a focused component that handles all file output operations, improving code organization by separating writing concerns from reading and other file utilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: writing data to files. They handle writing different data types (CharSequence, String, byte arrays) to files with various options for charset encoding and append mode. All methods create files if they don't exist and handle the low-level details of file output operations.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "file writing" domain. They represent a cohesive subsystem for output operations, providing a unified interface for writing textual and binary data to the filesystem. The methods form natural overload families (write, writeStringToFile, writeByteArrayToFile) with consistent parameter patterns and behavior.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates file writing concerns from other file operations (reading, copying, etc.)
- Code Reusability: File writing logic can be reused across different applications without pulling in the entire FileUtils class
- Complexity Reduction: Reduces the size and complexity of the original FileUtils class by removing 18 related methods
- Testability: File writing operations can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate output concerns from input and other file operations. The extracted class will have high cohesion (all methods write to files) and low coupling (minimal dependencies, only requiring newOutputStream method). This aligns with the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class focused solely on file output operations.

suggestions.name: FileSystemNavigator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for navigating and discovering files within the file system. They provide various traversal strategies (listing, iterating, streaming) with different filtering and recursion options, along with supporting validation and conversion utilities. Extracting them creates a focused component dedicated to file system navigation operations.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively handle file system navigation, traversal, and discovery operations. They provide various ways to list, iterate, stream, and find files and directories within the file system. The core responsibility is abstracting and simplifying file system traversal operations with different filtering, recursion, and output format options.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "file system traversal and discovery" domain. They share common concepts like directory navigation, file filtering, recursive traversal, and converting between different collection types (arrays, collections, iterators, streams). The URL-to-File conversion methods also support file discovery from URL sources. Helper methods like `checkExists`, `requireDirectoryExists`, and various conversion utilities support the main traversal operations.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates file system traversal logic from other file operations (copying, moving, etc.)
- Code Reusability: File traversal operations can be reused across different contexts without pulling in unrelated file utilities
- Complexity Reduction: Removes a large cluster of related methods from the main FileUtils class, making it more focused
- Testability: File traversal logic can be tested independently with mock file systems

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate file system traversal concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion (all related to finding/navigating files) and can operate with low coupling to other file operations. This creates a focused component following the Single Responsibility Principle.
